title: Deaths from volcanic eruptions
subtitle: Includes direct deaths, in addition to secondary impacts such as a tsunami or earthquake triggered by an eruption. Due to data availability, recent data will be more complete than older historical estimates.
caption:
Deaths from volcanic eruptions, World, 1570 to 2024
Includes direct deaths, in addition to secondary impacts such as a tsunami or earthquake triggered by an eruption. Due to data availability, recent data will be more complete than older historical estimates.

The bar chart records annual global fatalities from volcanic eruptions between 1570 and 2024, capturing both direct deaths and secondary impacts such as tsunamis or earthquakes triggered by eruptions. The vertical axis measures deaths from zero to 60,000, while the horizontal axis spans the timeline. The data shows a pattern of low annual mortality punctuated by infrequent, extreme spikes. The highest visible bar occurs in 1815, reaching the 60,000 mark. A second major spike appears in 1902, with deaths exceeding 40,000. Other significant peaks include 1883, where the bar rises above 30,000, and 1792, which shows approximately 15,000 deaths. The year 1985 displays a bar reaching roughly 23,000. Between these events, most years show bars near the baseline, often below 1,000 deaths. The subtitle notes that recent data is more complete than older historical estimates, a factor visible in the density of recorded events in the 20th and 21st centuries compared to the sparse reporting in the 16th and 17th centuries. The distribution remains uneven across the timeline, with clusters of activity in the late 18th century, the late 19th century, and the early 20th century. The visual record shows that while volcanic activity is continuous, the human cost concentrates in specific years where eruption magnitude or secondary effects result in mass casualties.
